# Harley-Davidson (HOG) — Interrogator Forensic Dossier

*Eight consecutive earnings calls reviewed: Q2-2024 (2024-07-25) through Q1-2026 (2026-05-05). Cross-referenced against the screener's fiscal-year trajectory (2021–2025), TTM/latest-quarter overrides, and the live net-debt/insider/segment corrections.*

---

## 1. NARRATIVE ARC

The story over these eight quarters is a textbook **slow-motion descent from "managed cyclical trough" to "structural reset under new management,"** with one large balance-sheet event bolted on in the middle.

- **Q2/Q3-2024 — "Hardwire is working, the cycle is the only problem."** Jochen Zeitz's framing is that the *business* is fine and the *macro* is the villain: "the continued impact of the high interest rate environment affecting our industry, and in particular big ticket consumer discretionary sectors" (Q2-24). The hero product is the redesigned Touring platform ("Harley-Davidson touring being up 5.3 percentage points in share and over 11% in unit growth," Q2-24). The narrative actively rebuts the bear case on demographics, with a whole slide dedicated to "the average age of our customers… is about 45… has not moved significantly in the last 10 years."
- **Q4-2024/Q1-2025 — "Two halves; defend the franchise; cut costs."** Tone shifts from offense to *preservation*. Management leans on "$257 million in productivity savings," "operating cash flow well over $1 billion in 2024, nearly a 40% increase," and starts withdrawing/qualifying guidance. By Q1-2025 (2025-05-01) guidance is **withdrawn entirely** — "we are withdrawing our previous 2025 guidance until there is more clarity over economy and tariff landscape" — and a brand-new financial-engineering thread appears: "we are evaluating an investment into HDFS."
- **Q2-2025 — THE PIVOT QUARTER (the whole thesis changes).** The call *opens* not with bikes but with the HDFS transaction: KKR + PIMCO take 9.8% of HDFS at "approximately 1.75x post-transaction book value," with a "$1.25 billion… distribution to HDI," "$450 million" debt paydown, and an "accelerate[d] $1 billion share buyback program… with the intention to purchase $500 million in the second half of '25." Management explicitly reframes the entire equity thesis as a **sum-of-parts value-unlock**: "this transaction implies that HDFS is trading at around 8x consensus operating income compared to peers at around 14x… The combined $1.75 billion of HDFS driven value compares to Harley-Davidson's current market cap of approximately $3 billion."
- **Q3-2025 — MANAGEMENT CHANGE.** Zeitz is gone; **Artie Starrs** (ex-Pizza Hut/Yum franchise operator, "having earned my motorcycle license this year") is CEO. The narrative resets again — from "Hardwire growth" to "fix the dealers first": "A healthy Harley depends on a healthy dealer network… dealer profitability." The honest tell: "As we've dug deeper than initially anticipated, it's become evident that the challenges are more significant."
- **Q4-2025/Q1-2026 — "2026 is a transition year; Back to the Bricks."** The new strategy is unveiled May-2026: affordability ladder ("$6,000" small-displacement bike, "starts with the 9… 14… 19… 24"), "enterprise profitability" (lifecycle value, not unit sale), and "$150 million plus" of 2027+ cost savings. Crucially management itself caps near-term expectations: "we view 2026 as a transition year… we expect margins to be under pressure in the near term as production runs below wholesale, creating operating deleverage."

**Growth drivers introduced then quietly demoted:** LiveWire EV (Q2-24 "pioneering the industry") → by Q1-25 "Harley-Davidson is evaluating all options for its investment in LiveWire" and capped at a $100M line of credit → by Q1-26 a one-line aside. The entry-level/small-displacement bike has been "in development since '21" and is *still* pre-launch in Q1-26 — a five-year-overdue catalyst.

---

## 2. CLAIMS vs FINANCIALS (forensic)

| Management claim | Quarter | What the financials show | Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|
| "Hardwire… reestablish our profitability"; "$400M cost productivity… overachieving by ~10%" | Q4-24 / Q2-25 | Revenue **5.84B→5.19B→4.47B** (FY23→24→25); net income **707M→455M→339M**; EPS **4.87→3.44→2.78**. Cost-out is real but is being *outrun* by volume collapse. | **Poorer than claimed** — productivity is a treadmill under a -13.8% YoY revenue line |
| "operating cash flow well over $1 billion in 2024, nearly a 40% increase" | Q4-24 | FY-FCF margin 9.3% on the annual line, but **FCF growth -52.1% YoY** and **TTM FCF just 44M** as of 2026-03-31. The 2024 cash strength was working-capital release from destocking, not earnings. | **True but transient** — 2024 OCF was inventory liquidation, now exhausted |
| HDFS "highest returning transportation-related captive finance company… 1.75x post-transaction book" | Q2-25 | The 1.75x mark is a genuine third-party validation; the $1.25B distribution + $500M buyback **did happen** (16.6M shares / 13.6% retired in 2025). | **TRUE and CORROBORATED** — the one claim that fully delivered |
| "clear path to growing HDFS operating income quickly towards pre-transaction levels in an asset-light manner" | Q2-25 | Q1-26: consolidated revenue **-12%, driven by HDFS revenue -54%**; HDFS normalized OI guided to only "~$120–130M going forward" (Q3-25) vs the ~$250M+ it used to earn. | **Over-promised** — "quickly towards pre-transaction levels" is contradicted by the company's own $120–130M run-rate |
| "double-digit margin in 2026… 15% [HDMC] target still achievable" | Q1-25 (Zeitz) | Q1-26 HDMC gross margin **25.3% (down from 29.1%)**; consolidated OI **$23M vs $160M** a year earlier; EPS **$0.22 vs $1.07**. New CEO now calls 2026 a "transition year" with margins "under pressure." | **ABANDONED** — the 15%/double-digit-2026 promise died with the prior CEO |
| Retail "green shoots" / "North American retail… up 14%" | Q4-25 / Q1-26 | Genuinely the first real positive: **Q1-26 NA retail +14%, US +16%, global +8%**, US 601cc+ share 36→38%. But on **-22% dealer inventory** and heavy promo — sell-through, not sell-in. | **TRUE but low-quality** — demand pulled forward by incentives into a destocked channel |
| Insider conviction ("Insider buy ratio 4.06") | scan | **Live correction:** open-market only = **3 buys (21,775sh) vs 4 sells (13,813sh)** — net buyers but tiny; the 4.06 is grant-inflated. | **Overstated** — real open-market signal is mildly positive, not a loud insider buy |

**Net:** the *balance-sheet* claims (HDFS value, buyback) delivered exactly as promised — that is the credible spine of this story. The *operating* claims (15% margin, HDFS earnings recovering "quickly," Hardwire restoring profitability) have serially missed, and management has had to retreat to "transition year" language. The narrative is **richer than the P&L trajectory justifies on operations, and exactly as rich as the balance sheet justified — but that balance-sheet event is now in the past.**

---

## 3. TONE & CONFIDENCE TRAJECTORY

**Trajectory: DECLINING then RE-BASING (low but stabilizing).**

- **Q2-24 (confident/offensive):** "we continue to believe there's meaningful growth potential for the company" — growth language, demographic defense, festival/brand color.
- **Q3-24 (defensive creeping in):** "an increasingly difficult global market environment"; "we are mindful of the external factors, including interest rate reductions… that are required to provide the industry with the needed tailwind." First explicit dependence on a macro rescue.
- **Q4-24/Q1-25 (defensive, guidance withdrawn):** "we were unable to achieve our original guidance of 2024"; "we are withdrawing our previous 2025 guidance." Language is now cost/cash/tariff-dominated.
- **Q2-25 (event-driven confidence — but it's financial, not operational):** bullish *only* about the HDFS deal ("a big win on all levels"); the underlying motorcycle business is "consolidated revenue… declined 19%."
- **Q3-25/Q4-25 (new-CEO candor):** Starrs is refreshingly blunt — "dealer health today is uneven"; "challenges are more significant" than thought; "2026 as a transition year." This is *lower* confidence on the numbers but *higher* credibility on tone.
- **Q1-26 (cautious optimism, earned):** "we're pleased with our performance this quarter" — but immediately hedged: "we remain mindful of the global consumer discretionary landscape, which remains uneven."

**Specificity over time: DECREASING then absent.** The company went from full unit/margin guidance (Q2-24) to *no guidance at all* (Q1-25 onward), and as of Q1-26 still has not reinstated formal financial guidance — deferring everything to the May strategy day, which then delivered a multi-year *qualitative* plan rather than dated numeric targets.

---

## 4. GUIDANCE CREDIBILITY

**Reliability score: LOW-to-MEDIUM (improving under new management).**

- **The damning record (Zeitz era):** FY2024 original guidance **missed** ("unable to achieve our original guidance"). The "balanced retail/wholesale by end of '24" promise (Q2-24) slipped. The "15% HDMC margin… double-digit 2026" target (Q1-25) was effectively **withdrawn** within two quarters by his successor. Guidance was then **suspended entirely** (Q1-25) and remains suspended through Q1-26. The earnings-beat rate in the financials — **3 of 7 quarters (43%)** — independently confirms a sub-coin-flip delivery record.
- **The one thing they over-delivered on:** the HDFS transaction and the $500M buyback were promised in Q2-25 and **executed on schedule** (closed Oct-30-2025; buyback completed in 2025). When Harley commits to a *financial/transactional* action it has been reliable; when it commits to an *operating* outcome (volume, margin) it has not.
- **Sandbagging pattern?** No — the opposite. This is a serial **over-promise / under-deliver** on operations, now being corrected by a new CEO who is deliberately *under-promising* ("transition year," "margins under pressure") to reset the bar. That re-basing is healthy but means there is **no credible forward numeric guidance to underwrite** today.

---

## 5. ANALYST PRESSURE POINTS

- **"When is LiveWire profitable / why keep funding it?"** (Brandon Rollé Q4-24; Jaime Katz Q4-24). Management deflected for years ("committed… but we need to see improvements") before quietly ring-fencing it at a $100M LOC and ~$59M loss. Analysts were right to push; it was a multi-year value leak.
- **The entry-level / small-displacement bike** (James Hardiman Q2-25: "what's been elusive to Harley historically… an entry-level bike that's both popular *and profitable*"). Management has promised "$6,000" profitable small bikes "since '21" — five years and still not launched. This is the single most-pushed, least-delivered topic.
- **HDFS go-forward earnings** (Joe Altobello Q3-25: "still thinking around… $120 million to $130 million going forward?"). Management confirmed the *de-rating of earnings power* almost in passing — the SoP unlock came at the cost of permanently lower captive-finance income.
- **Demographics / aging customer** (Tristan Thomas-Martin Q2-24; Jaime Katz repeatedly). Management's age-45 slide is a defensive set-piece; the recurring analyst skepticism is the real signal that the core-customer cohort is the structural overhang.
- **Margin bridge / "what gets you back to expansion"** (Jaime Katz Q3-25, Q1-26). Management's answer is circular — "the inverse of what you've been seeing in 2025" — i.e., they need volume to come back, which is exactly the uncertain variable.

**The deflection that later became bad news:** every quarter analysts asked for a 2025/2026 numeric guide; management deferred. The deferral *was* the bad news — there was no number management was willing to stand behind.

---

## 6. RED FLAGS / GREEN FLAGS

🔴 **Revenue in secular decline, not just cyclical:** -13.8% YoY, **-8.1% 3-yr CAGR**; EPS 5-yr CAGR **-9.8%**. Four straight down years on the top line.
🔴 **Earnings power permanently lowered by the very deal that unlocked cash:** HDFS revenue **-54%** (Q1-26), HDFS OI reset to ~$120–130M from ~$250M+. The SoP "unlock" cannibalized recurring income.
🔴 **TTM FCF collapsed to $44M** (from a >$1B-OCF 2024) and **latest-Q EPS -79% YoY** — the cash engine that funded the buyback has sputtered.
🔴 **The $1B buyback target was ABANDONED** at the May-2026 strategy day ("will no longer pursue the $1 billion target") — removing the mechanical demand that supported the stock post-deal.
🔴 **Guidance withdrawn for ~5 consecutive quarters** and not reinstated; no dated numeric targets even at the strategy reveal.
🔴 **CEO + strategy turnover mid-stream** — Hardwire/Hardwire II (Zeitz) discarded for "Back to the Bricks" (Starrs); the new entry-level/affordability bet is unproven and the prior management's flagship targets are dead.
🔴 **Tariff exposure** — $45M hit in Q1-26 alone, full-year **$75–90M** headwind; ~25% non-US supply content into a 145%-China-tariff world (Q1-25).

🟢 **Q1-26 is the first genuine demand inflection:** NA retail **+14%**, US **+16%**, global **+8%**, US 601cc+ share **36→38%**, EU share 2→4% — real, broad-based, and *outperforming* a weak industry.
🟢 **Channel is clean:** dealer inventory **-22% YoY**, two-thirds now current model year (vs <½ a year ago) — the destocking that crushed wholesale is largely *done*, setting up easier 2026 wholesale comps (management guides "ship more… in Q2 and Q4").
🟢 **The HDFS deal delivered exactly as promised** — $1.25B distribution, $450M debt cut, $500M buyback (13.6% of shares) — a rare fully-kept capital-allocation commitment, and a hard third-party 1.75x book mark on the finance arm.
🟢 **New CEO credibility:** Starrs is candid ("challenges are more significant than anticipated," "transition year"), franchise-operator pedigree fits a dealer-economics problem, and he is deliberately re-basing expectations rather than defending a doomed target.
🟢 **Net-cash balance sheet** (live recompute: ~$1.6B cash at HDI; manufacturing net debt very low after the $450M paydown) — solvency is not the question; Piotroski 7/9, Altman-Z 2.2.

---

## 7. HIDDEN SIGNALS

- **The framing of the equity thesis shifted from earnings to break-up value** in Q2-25 — management *itself* started talking like a sum-of-parts activist ("HDFS trading at ~8x… peers at ~14x," "$1.75B HDFS value vs ~$3B market cap"). When management makes the SoP argument *for* you, the easy alpha is usually being harvested *by the deal itself* — which is exactly what happened (deal closed, cash returned).
- **"Enterprise profitability" is a tell:** Starrs (Q1-26) redefines success as "the total lifecycle value of a motorcycle across parts, service, and financing rather than just the initial sale." That is the language of a company that has **given up on unit-volume growth as the primary lever** and is pivoting to monetizing the installed base — a structurally lower-growth, annuity framing.
- **From "premium/highest-MSRP" to "affordability/$6,000 bike":** the brand's entire positioning argument inverted across these calls. In Q2-24 the high MSRP was a *feature* explaining the age-45 customer; by Q1-26 affordability is the *strategy*. That is a quiet admission the premium-only model has hit its addressable-market ceiling.
- **New voices, blunter messaging:** Root's promotion to "CFO and President of Commercial," and Starrs replacing Zeitz, coincide with the disappearance of aspirational targets and the arrival of "transition year." The new team is managing *down* expectations they did not set.
- **The buyback-abandonment language is diplomatic but decisive:** "decided that it will no longer pursue the $1 billion target… align… with Back to the Bricks." Translation: with FCF at TTM $44M, they cannot fund a large buyback *and* the affordability-product reinvestment — and they chose reinvestment.

---

## 8. CAPITAL-ALLOCATION VERDICT (for the director)

**Classification: PIVOTING (financially de-risked, operationally unproven), bottoming on operations.**

Harley has executed a clean, value-accretive **balance-sheet** pivot — monetized HDFS at a credible 1.75x book, returned ~$1.75B of value (distribution + buyback that retired 13.6% of shares), and cut debt — and is now bottoming operationally, with Q1-26 the first real demand green shoot on a cleaned-up channel. The new CEO is candid and the franchise/dealer-economics diagnosis is sound. **But the load-bearing catalyst that re-rated this name has already FIRED:** the deal is closed, the cash is distributed, the buyback is done *and the residual $1B target is cancelled*. What remains is a multi-year, qualitative "Back to the Bricks" turnaround with **no reinstated guidance, structurally lower HDFS earnings (-54% revenue), TTM FCF of just $44M, and a top line still in secular decline.**

**The single most important thing the director must weigh before sizing:** *the special-situation alpha here is spent.* You are no longer underwriting a sum-of-parts unlock — that happened in 2025 and the stock (~$24.4) already reflects it (analyst consensus PT clusters $21.6–$25.5, i.e. **no upside**). What is left to underwrite is an **operating turnaround of a structurally-declining discretionary brand**, where the only *new* positive (Q1-26 retail) was incentive-driven into a destocked channel, and where the cash to fund both the reinvestment and any return of capital is currently thin. Treat this as a **post-catalyst, show-me operating story**, not a live special-sit — and size accordingly.

CREDIBILITY_SCORE: 3 | TRAJECTORY: PIVOTING

bull_thesis: HOG is a 122-year-old iconic brand trading at ~7.5x P/FCF and below several conservative intrinsic methods (EPV +23%, Graham +33%, OwnerEarnings +61%) after a brutal three-year de-rating. The bull case is a candid, competently-run turnaround atop a real completed value-unlock. (1) The HDFS/KKR/PIMCO transaction CLOSED (Oct-30-2025) at a validating 1.75x post-transaction book, distributed ~$1.25B to HDI (~40% of the then-$3B market cap), funded a ~$450M debt paydown and a ~$500M buyback (shrinking the share count into the trough), and turned HDFS into a capital-light fee/servicing annuity (~$120-130M normalized op income) that de-risks the balance sheet. (2) New CEO Artie Starrs has re-based the story honestly: Q1-26 delivered NA retail +14% / global +8% and US 601cc+ share to 38% (+2pts), with global dealer inventory down 22% YoY and a healthier model-year mix entering riding season -- genuine demand-side green shoots. (3) The 'Back to the Bricks' plan (announced May-5-2026) targets $350M+ HDMC EBITDA and 10-12% HDMC EBITDA margin by 2027, dealers to double profitability in 2026, and $150M+ annual run-rate savings from 2027 -- if even partially delivered, normalized earnings power sits well above the depressed $1.76 TTM EPS. (4) Affordability product (sub-$10k Nightster repricing already lifting RevMax +16% NA; $6k small-displacement bike + iconic Cruiser from 2H-26/27) addresses the one growing segment. (5) A US-centric manufacturing footprint becomes a relative tariff advantage if import duties on foreign bikes stick. Insider open-market buying is modestly net positive (3 buys/21,775sh vs 4 sells/13,813sh) and Piotroski is 7/9. The bull needs: retail momentum to persist past promotional stimulus, margin to recover off the 25.3% Q1-26 trough as production re-balances, and the 2027 EBITDA targets to prove credible.
bear_thesis: The two events this stock traded on -- the HDFS transaction and the strategic-plan reveal -- have BOTH already fired, and what is left is a secularly shrinking, tariff-exposed discretionary manufacturer at full price. (1) Revenue has fallen 5.84B (2023) -> 5.19B (2024) -> 4.47B (2025), -13.8% YoY and -8.1% 3-yr CAGR; EPS 4.87 -> 3.44 -> 2.78 (-17.6% 3-yr CAGR) with TTM diluted EPS now just $1.76 and latest-Q EPS YoY -79%. (4) The post-unlock balance sheet is a NET LIABILITY (+$395M net debt at 2026-03-31): the $1.25B is spent, not a hidden hoard. consensus_delta: For a deep_value name the question is whether the street is pricing terminal decline into stable earnings power -- and here it largely is NOT mispriced. Consensus target ~$25.45 vs price $25.64 = ~0% upside; the analyst base (5 buy / 2 sell, Neutral overall) already understands both the HDFS unlock and the Q1-26 retail bounce. The genuine delta is small and two-sided: bulls over-extrapolate the Q1-26 '+14% NA / +8% global' as a clean inflection when it was bought with 380bps of gross-margin (29.1% -> 25.3%) via incentives -- so the street's optimists are wrong that profitability is inflecting (it isn't yet). forcing_function: There is no remaining hard-dated, binding, asymmetric forcing function. The two that existed have FIRED: HDFS close (Oct-30-2025) and the May-5-2026 'Back to the Bricks' reveal. The next scheduled events are soft/far-dated: Q2-2026 earnings (~46 days out) as the first test of whether retail momentum survives without promo and whether margin recovers off 25.3%, and the 2027 $350M+ HDMC EBITDA / $150M savings targets which are a 12-24 month execution proof, not an event. Per the regime doc, this is exactly the kind of single-binary, far-dated execution story (no spin, no forced seller, no distressed fulcrum) that the current tilt deprioritizes.
valley_of_death: Concrete 3-9 month drags: (1) 2026 is self-described as a 'transition year' with 'margins under pressure' from operating deleverage as production runs below wholesale to clear touring inventory -- Q1-26 already showed op income $23M vs $160M. (2) A full-year tariff drag of $75-90M ($45M hit in Q1-26 alone; +~$25M YoY headwind per mgmt). (3) HDFS revenue down 54% YoY as the capital-light model laps -- optically ugly comps through 2026. None of these is solvency-threatening (Altman-Z 2.2, net debt only $395M), but they cap any near-term re-rate and create downside-to-$22 risk before any 2027 payoff.
positioning_washout: Limited forced-selling dynamic remaining. HOG is a former dividend/value holding, but the big mechanical events have already cleared: HDFS proceeds funded the buyback (a support, not an overhang), and the company has not signaled a dividend cut on these calls. moderator_conclusion: PASS (verdict C, conviction 2). Applying the 2026-06 regime (late-cycle, fully-invested, structural-special-sits tilt; reward hard-dated catalysts inside the favorable window, penalize past/out-of-window catalysts and single-binary far-dated stories): HOG fails the catalyst test decisively. interrogator_dossier:
# Harley-Davidson (HOG) — Interrogator Forensic Dossier

*Five consecutive quarterly calls: Q1-2025 (2025-05-01), Q2-2025 (2025-07-30), Q3-2025 (2025-11-04), Q4/FY-2025 (2026-02-10), Q1-2026 (2026-05-05). Two CEOs across the window (Jochen Zeitz → Artie Starrs, effective Oct-2025). Source: FMP transcripts + screener financials.*

## 1. NARRATIVE ARC

The story bent sharply across these five quarters, and the bend is the whole point.

**Acts I–II (Q1–Q2 2025, the Zeitz "Hardwire" endgame).** The opening narrative is *managed decline plus a financial-engineering rabbit out of the hat*. Q1 is grim operationally — "Global retail sales were down 21% … down 24% in North America," guidance withdrawn over tariffs ("withdrawing our previous 2025 guidance until there is more clarity") — but the emphasis is on a coming *value unlock*: management floats HDFS as a crown jewel ("the highest returning transportation-related captive finance company in America") and signals a transaction. Q2 delivers it: the KKR/PIMCO partnership, sold at "approximately 1.75x post-transaction book valuation," generating "$1.25 billion … to HDI, representing around 40% of our current market cap." Zeitz frames the whole company as mispriced: "$1.75 billion of HDFS driven value compares to Harley-Davidson's current market cap of approximately $3 billion." A second future-growth pillar is introduced here too — a profitable entry-level "small displacement" bike "starting at $6,000" and an "iconic Cruiser," both "for the first time … that we can actually make money on."

**Act III (Q3 2025, the handoff).** New CEO Artie Starrs arrives. The narrative pivots hard from *financial unlock* to *operational repair*: "dealer health," inventory reduction "particularly in the touring and CVO segments," promotions, e-commerce reset. The HDFS transaction is now a closed mechanical event being settled in tranches, not a thesis. Growth language recedes; "transition," "reset," "dealer profitability" enter.

## 5. ANALYST PRESSURE POINTS

- **"What's the cadence / when do margins inflect?"** (Tristan/BMO Q1; Katz/Morningstar Q3 & Q4; Hardiman/Citi Q4). Repeatedly deflected to "we'll guide in 2026" / "more in May." Management would not bridge wholesale growth to operating income — Root's Q4 answer is a tautology: margin expands via "the inverse of what you've been seeing in 2025." This is the recurring non-answer.
- **The SDB profitability mechanism** (Hardiman, Q2). Zeitz explicitly declined: "I don't want to go into specifics of how we feel we've accomplished that." The single biggest growth claim has no disclosed unit economics.
- **HDFS normalized run-rate** (Altobello/Raymond James, Q3). Pinned management to ~$120-130M go-forward — a *halving* analysts extracted, not volunteered.
- **Used-vs-new pricing spread** (Rollé/Loop, Q4). A leading indicator of brand pricing power and trade-down risk; management spun it positively but the question itself flags that promotional intensity is eroding the new-bike value equation.

Topics analysts pushed that *became* bad news: inventory ("we don't envision loading dealers," Q1 → "touring overhang remains pronounced," Q4) and margin cadence (deferred all year → 72% Q1'26 miss).

---

## 6. RED FLAGS / GREEN FLAGS

**🔴 Red flags**
- 🔴 **Guidance withdrawn Q1'25 and never restored** the entire window — a multi-quarter visibility blackout.
- 🔴 **Core revenue in structural decline:** FY21 $5.34B → FY25 $4.47B; EPS $4.96 (FY22 peak) → $2.78 (FY25) → TTM $1.76. 3-yr revenue CAGR -8.1%, EPS CAGR -17.6%.

- 🔴 **CEO turnover** mid-turnaround (Zeitz → Starrs, Oct'25); strategy reset to "May."
- 🔴 **Net debt +$395M** despite the cash infusion — the balance sheet is not the fortress the "$1.6B cash" headline implies once $2.2B gross debt and the €700M note are counted.

**🟢 Green flags**
- 🟢 **Buyback executed** — 26.8M shares retired (~$726M), share count to ~105M; genuine per-share support at a low multiple.
- 🟢 **HDFS print validated the hidden-asset thesis** at 1.75x book / implied 8x op income vs peers ~14x — the SoP discount is *evidenced by a real transaction*, not just a model.
- 🟢 **NA retail inflected positive** in Q4'25 (Dec acceleration), and Q1'26 global retail +8% — early demand stabilization.
- 🟢 **Insider open-market buying** (3 buys / 21,775 sh vs 4 sells / 13,813 sh) — modest net conviction, not grant noise.
- 🟢 **Tariff asymmetry could turn favorable** — US-built, US-supply-chain footprint (~75%) becomes a relative advantage if import tariffs on foreign bikes stick; new-bike loan-interest deductibility (sub-$10k) is a demand sweetener.

## 7. HIDDEN SIGNALS

- **Terminology shift from "growth/unlock" to "health/discipline/affordability."** Starrs uses "dealer health," "affordability," "trade down," "customer number one (the dealer)" — the vocabulary of a *franchise operator managing decline*, not a growth CEO. He is "new to the sport of riding… earned my motorcycle license this year" — an outsider running a culturally insular brand.
- **The "affordability" admission is a tell on the core demographic:** "consumers who are sort of trading down rather than trading up" (Q3). The $6k SDB pivot concedes the legacy $20k+ touring buyer is aging out / priced out — a demand-mix problem, not just a cycle.
- **Capital allocation pivoted from offense to defense within one year:** Q2'25 "$300M for growth opportunities" → Q4'25 the only named capital priority is *paying off the €700M note*. Deleveraging, not investing.
- **LiveWire quietly abandoned** — from a strategic EV bet to "$100M line of credit, no more," with Harley "evaluating all options." A written-down option, not a driver.
- **"Production below wholesale" (Q4)** is an accounting tell that *under-absorption* will depress FY26 margins regardless of retail — the deleverage is mechanical and largely already baked.

---

## 8. CAPITAL-ALLOCATION VERDICT (for the director)

**Classification: PIVOTING (toward DETERIORATING core, propped by a now-spent financial catalyst).**

This is a turnaround that has already played its two highest-value cards — the HDFS monetization (Q2'25) and the May'26 "Back to the Bricks" strategy reveal — *both of which have now fired*. The buyback is real and the SoP discount to HDFS is transaction-validated, but the operating core is in **secular, not merely cyclical, decline**: an aging core ridership, a $20k+ touring product the customer is trading *down* from, revenue down a third in four years, ROIC at 5.3%, and TTM FCF collapsed to $44M. Management itself calls 2026 a "transition year" with margins "under pressure."

**The single most important thing the director must weigh:** the value-unlock catalysts are *behind* the stock, not ahead of it. At ~$25.6 (≈where it traded pre- and post-the May pop), the market has already absorbed both the HDFS print and the strategy plan; analyst PTs sit at $23.60–$28, i.e. *no upside to consensus*. Any remaining edge is purely the SoP-vs-secular-decline tension — is the brand+HDFS+segment value a floor under a melting core, or is the core melting faster than the buyback can offset? Size for a *value-trap* risk, not a catalyst.

**Economic moat:** intangible-brand (the Harley marque, the HOG community, the exclusive dealer network — genuinely a switching/affinity moat). But the EVIDENCE says it is **ERODING**, not wide: ROIC 5.3% (below WACC), gross margin contracting from prior peaks, net margin 7.6% and falling, revenue -13.8% YoY. A real brand throwing off sub-WACC returns on a shrinking base is an eroding moat by definition. **Secular threat:** the core threat is **demographic demand decay** of US heavyweight cruiser ridership (aging core buyer, weak youth adoption) — best mapped to the *energy-transition-loser* tail only loosely (ICE-linked, but the live driver is demographic/affordability, not EV substitution per se). It is **material** but not strictly *terminal* on a 5-year view: the brand and HDFS provide a hard asset floor, and the SDB/affordability pivot is a credible (if unproven) defense. The cheapness is therefore **partly a fear-print over a real asset floor, partly a structurally-shrinking base** — a value situation with a genuine value-trap tail, NOT a clean mispricing.

interrogator_dossier:
# HOG (Harley-Davidson, Inc.) — Forensic Dossier

*Interrogator pass. Transcripts: Q1'25 (2025-05-01), Q2'25 (2025-07-30), Q3'25 (2025-11-04), Q4'25 (2026-02-10). Cross-referenced against the screener financials and live web verification through June 2026.*

## 1. NARRATIVE ARC

The story across these four quarters is a **regime change in the narrative itself**, not just in the numbers. It moves through three distinct phases:

**Phase 1 — "Macro is doing this to us" (Q1'25, Zeitz).** The opening call is defensive and externalizing. Retail down 21% globally / 24% North America, "driven by historically low levels of consumer confidence in the uncertain macro environment." Guidance is *withdrawn* ("we therefore are withdrawing our previous 2025 guidance until there is more clarity over economy and tariff landscape"). The growth driver introduced is hope-flavored: a future entry-level small-displacement bike (SDB) "starting next year" and tariff-mitigation theater ("working literally 24/7"). The bull hook is that a U.S.-centric manufacturing base "could be a competitive advantage" if import tariffs stick — a conditional, not a fact.

**Phase 2 — "We just unlocked the balance sheet" (Q2'25, Zeitz).** The narrative pivots hard to a **financial-engineering catalyst**: the HDFS partnership with KKR and PIMCO at ~1.75x post-transaction book value, a $1.25B distribution to HDI (≈40% of market cap), $450M debt paydown, and an accelerated $500M buyback in H2'25. Management explicitly frames it as a sum-of-parts value-unlock: "$1.75 billion of HDFS driven value compares to Harley-Davidson's current market cap of approximately $3 billion… HDFS is trading at around 8x consensus operating income compared to peers at around 14x." This is the high-water mark of the bull case — and it is a *one-time* realization, not a recurring engine. The SDB at a $6,000 price point and a "profitable" entry Cruiser are dangled as the volume future.

**Phase 3 — "We have work to do; this is a reset" (Q3'25 & Q4'25, new CEO Artie Starrs).** A CEO change lands mid-stream (Starrs, ex-franchise operator, "earned my motorcycle license this year"). The language inverts from value-unlock to **turnaround/repair**: dealer health "uneven," touring inventory "overhang remains pronounced," promotions to clear stock, e-commerce walked back, fuel-facility penalties suspended. By Q4'25 management openly calls **2026 "a transition year"** with "margins under pressure in the near term as production runs below wholesale, creating operating deleverage." Guidance stays withdrawn. The forward catalyst is pushed out again — a full strategic plan deferred to **May 2026**.

Net arc: the thesis *contracted* from "premium-brand growth franchise" → "cheap stub after a financial-services unlock" → "multi-year operational turnaround of a structurally shrinking core." The growth drivers that were quietly dropped or deferred: 2025 unit guidance (withdrawn and never restored), LiveWire (de-funded, "evaluating all options"), and the SDB (still not in market). The driver that was *added* is balance-sheet repair and dealer-network triage.

## 2. CLAIMS vs FINANCIALS (forensic)

| Management claim | Where | The hard numbers say | Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|
| "HDFS… class-leading returns… 1.75x book; HDFS undervalued vs peers at 14x" | Q2'25 | The transaction was real and closed (Q4'25): $1.25B distribution, $500M buyback, $450M debt paydown. But it is **non-recurring** and partly **dilutes the future**: HDFS keeps only ~1/3 of originations; consolidated HDFS operating income fell ~65% post-restructuring in Q1'26. | **TRUE but spent** — value realized, not value pending |
| "Cost control… better than expected bottom line" | Q1'25 (12.1% HDI op margin) | The whole trajectory is downhill: rev 5.84B (2023) → 5.19B (2024) → 4.47B (2025); EPS 4.87 → 3.44 → 2.78; FCF growth −52% YoY; TTM FCF just **44M**; latest-Q EPS YoY **−79%**. | **Narrative richer than numbers** — quarter-level cost wins mask a collapsing base |
| "Affordable, *profitable* entry-level SDB… significant unlock" | Q2'25 | Not in market; "in development since '21"; HOG has *never* made money in entry/cruiser ("never been a profitable business for many decades"). No financial line supports it yet. | **Unproven hope** — load-bearing for the bull, zero evidence in the financials |
| "Tariffs could be a competitive advantage" | Q1'25 | Q4'25 admits a **$25M YoY tariff headwind** into 2026; no offsetting benefit quantified. EU manufacturing gap is a *negative*. | **Reversed** — the optimistic spin became a quantified cost |
| "Inventory discipline… down 17% globally" | Q4'25 | Genuinely achieved (beat the 10% target). But the *method* — undershipping + promotions — is exactly what crushed margins: Q1'26 op income −85%, op margin 2% vs 12.1%. | **TRUE but self-inflicted earnings hit** |
| "Net cash / strong balance sheet" framing | implicit | Live recompute (2026-03-31): **NET DEBT 395M** (2.20B debt − 1.81B cash). This is a liability, not net cash. | **Misleading if taken at face value** |

The forensic core: every *operational* claim that can be checked against the financials is either deteriorating (revenue, EPS, FCF, margins) or self-inflicted (margin collapse from inventory discipline). The only claim that proved both true and value-additive — the HDFS unlock — is a **one-time event that has already happened and already been distributed**.

## 3. TONE & CONFIDENCE TRAJECTORY

**Trajectory: DECLINING, then RESETTING to humble.**

- **Q1'25 (Zeitz):** defensive, externalizing — "withdrawing our previous 2025 guidance"; "difficult to predict what policies may impact customers." Confidence low, blame outward.
- **Q2'25 (Zeitz):** a *spike* of confidence around the HDFS deal — "a big win on all levels," "major value unlock over time." This is the most offensive (growth/value) language in the set, but it is about a financial transaction, not the core motorcycle business.
- **Q3'25 (Starrs):** humble and diagnostic — "we have work to do to strengthen that foundation," "dealer health is not optional," "disappointed by the results of the Motor Company." New-CEO honesty replaces sales-pitch.
- **Q4'25 (Starrs):** explicitly defensive on the operating business — "2026 as a transition year," "margins to be under pressure," "operating deleverage," but with a credibility-building "green shoots" framing (NA retail +YoY in Q4, accelerating into December).

The shift from **offensive (Q2 value-unlock)** to **defensive (Q3-Q4 reset/efficiency)** is unambiguous. Guidance specificity *decreased* over the period (withdrawn and kept withdrawn through Q4'25; only a May-2026 plan promised). The new CEO is more specific about *problems* and *actions* but deliberately vague about *numbers* ("we're certainly not guiding on 2026").

## 4. GUIDANCE CREDIBILITY

**Reliability: LOW-to-MEDIUM.**

The defining fact is that **management withdrew 2025 guidance in Q1'25 and never reinstated it** — they cannot deliver against a number they refused to give. The screener corroborates a weak delivery record: **beat 3/7 quarters (43%)**, and the most recent prints are ugly (latest-Q EPS YoY −79%; TTM EPS 1.76 vs 2.78 fiscal-year). The HDFS-transaction guidance, by contrast, was delivered as promised and on schedule (announced Q2'25, all three components signed/closed by end-October 2025, distribution and buyback executed) — so management is **credible on discrete corporate transactions** and **not credible on operating forecasts**. There is no sandbagging pattern; if anything the operating misses were genuine (Q1'26 op income −85% surprised to the downside). The one forward number they did volunteer — Q4'25's "~flat HDMC operating income in '26 at the midpoint, with a $25M tariff headwind" and "$150M annual run-rate savings starting 2027" — is unverified and dated to a future they keep deferring.

## 5. ANALYST PRESSURE POINTS

- **2026 guidance / cadence:** repeatedly pushed (Hardiman, Wagner, Altobello, Katz) — management deflects every time ("not guiding on 2026," "more when we're together next quarter"). This recurring non-answer later became the Q4'25 admission that 2026 is a loss-pressured transition year.
- **HDFS normalized run-rate:** Altobello pins them on "$120-130M going forward" — management confirms a steep earnings *curve down* post-transaction, conceding the unit's standalone earnings power shrinks (the price of the capital-light model).
- **SDB profitability:** Hardiman presses on *how* an entry-level bike can finally be profitable when "previous generations of Harley management" failed — Zeitz refuses specifics ("I don't want to go into specifics of how we feel we've accomplished that"). A load-bearing claim defended with conviction but **no disclosed mechanism**.
- **Margin recovery path:** Katz asks repeatedly when HDMC margins return to "high single-digit" — punted entirely to the May-2026 plan ("tune in then").
- **Used-vs-new price spread:** Rollé probes whether promotions permanently compress the value of the brand — management spins it positively (used values "stabilizing"), but the structural worry (discounting erodes premium positioning) is real and unaddressed.

The pattern: analysts keep asking for *forward numbers* and *the SDB mechanism*; management keeps deferring to May 2026. The deferral itself is the signal.

## 6. RED FLAGS / GREEN FLAGS

🔴 **Revenue / EPS in secular descent:** rev −13.8% YoY, −8.1% 3-yr CAGR; EPS −9.8% 5-yr CAGR; FCF −52% YoY, TTM FCF only 44M. The core is shrinking, not pausing.
🔴 **Margin collapse is the *plan*:** Q1'26 op margin 2% (vs 12.1%); management says 2026 margins "under pressure" *by design* (undershipping). Worse before better, with no dated "better."
🔴 **Guidance withdrawn for >12 months** — a multi-quarter visibility void.
🔴 **CEO turnover mid-turnaround** (Zeitz → Starrs) with the new leader new to the sport itself; strategy reset to May 2026.
🔴 **Net debt 395M**, not net cash; €700M note to be repaid; ROIC 5.3% (below cost of capital).
🔴 **Aging core demographic + heavyweight-cruiser secular volume decline** — the SDB/entry pivot is an admission the core franchise is structurally challenged.
🔴 **The big catalysts have already fired** (HDFS close Q4'25; "Back to the Bricks" plan May 5, 2026) — re-rate spent.

🟢 **HDFS unlock executed cleanly** — $1.25B in, $500M buyback, $450M debt down; proves the parts are worth more than the whole *and* that this team executes transactions.
🟢 **Inventory discipline delivered** — global dealer inventory −17% (beat target), setting up cleaner 2026 comps.

## 7. HIDDEN SIGNALS

- **Vocabulary inversion:** Q1-Q2'25 vocabulary is "unlock / value / premium valuation / growth." Q3-Q4'25 vocabulary is "reset / dealer health / affordability / transition / under pressure / deleverage." The brand stopped talking like a growth story and started talking like a restructuring.
- **LiveWire quietly euthanized:** Q1'25 "evaluating all options… does not plan to provide additional investments beyond the $100M line"; by Q4'25 it is excluded from the $150M savings program and barely mentioned. A growth bet retired without ceremony.
- **The new CEO's tell:** Starrs reframes the customer — "**our dealers are customer number one**" — a channel-repair posture, not an end-consumer-demand posture. He is fixing distribution, not igniting demand. Honest, but it concedes the demand problem is not his near-term lever.
- **"Affordability" becomes the master frame:** by Q4'25 every product comment routes through price-laddering ("starts with the 9… 14… 19… 24"). For a premium brand, leaning into *affordability* is a tacit admission that pricing power is gone in the current cohort.
- **Capital allocation shifted from growth to repair:** the HDFS cash went to debt paydown + buyback + "demand-driving initiatives" (promotions), not to capacity or M&A — a defensive deployment.

## 8. CAPITAL-ALLOCATION VERDICT (for the director)

**Classification: PIVOTING** — from a premium-growth franchise into a balance-sheet-repaired, dealer-network-triage turnaround of a structurally shrinking core. The one clean value-realization event (HDFS) is *done and distributed*; the operating turnaround is unproven and self-described as a multi-year "transition."

**The single most important thing to weigh before sizing:** *the load-bearing catalysts have already fired.* The HDFS unlock that the bull case rests on closed in Q4'25 and the cash is already out the door (buyback + debt paydown); the "Back to the Bricks" strategic plan was unveiled May 5, 2026 and the stock has already run +45% in 90 days to ~$25.6 against a consensus target of ~$25.6 (no upside). What remains is a low-ROIC (5.3%), shrinking-revenue, margin-pressured business in a 2026 "transition year" — i.e. you would be buying *after* the re-rate, into the operating valley, on faith in an SDB that has never been profitable in HOG's history.

**Economic moat:** *intangible-brand* (the Harley name + exclusive dealer/HOG-community network is a genuine intangible). But the EVIDENCE marks it **ERODING, trend ERODING**: ROIC 5.3% (below WACC), gross margin 30.2% and *contracting*, net margin compressed, revenue −8% 3-yr CAGR, and management itself pivoting to *affordability* (pricing-power concession). A brand that must discount and chase a $6k entry bike to find growth is monetizing a fading franchise, not a widening one.

**Secular threat: MATERIAL (bordering terminal on the legacy core).** The heavyweight-cruiser demographic is aging out and younger cohorts are not replacing them at price — a structural volume decline, compounded by long-horizon **ICE/energy-transition** pressure on the powertrain (LiveWire's de-funding shows HOG cannot fund the EV answer). This is closer to a *structurally shrinking base* than a pure fear-print: the cheapness (7.5x P/FCF) is partly real value (the HDFS-unlocked balance sheet) and partly a **value-trap** on a melting core. Cheap on price, but the terminal-value question is unresolved and trending the wrong way.
